1: hattirshātāʾ — the Governor (10:1). tirshātāʾ is an Aramaic-Persian administrative loanword (from Old Persian tarsata = the feared one, the honorable one) that designates the Persian-appointed governor of a province. In Ezra-Nehemiah it is applied to Nehemiah (here and 8:9; cf. Ezra 2:63 where it designates the leader of the return). The titling of Nehemiah as tirshātāʾ at the head of the covenant document positions the civil authority alongside the priestly and Levitical representatives: the covenant renewal involves all social strata of the restoration community.
2: Śᵉrayāh ʿAzaryāh (10:2). The priestly family list opens ch 10:2-8 — the Yah-bearing names (compounded with YHWH) dominate: Seraiah (YHWH is prince/ruler), Azariah (YHWH has helped), Jeremiah (YHWH exalts). The priestly signatory order mirrors the genealogical framework of Neh 12 and Ezra 2 — the families certified as legitimately of Aaronic descent in the return. Their signing establishes priestly co-authorization of the covenant.
3: Pashḥûr ʾAmarYāh (10:3). Pashhur is one of the priestly families that recurs across Ezra-Nehemiah (Ezra 2:38; Neh 7:41; 11:12) — an ancient priestly lineage. Amariah (YHWH has spoken/said) is likewise a recurring priestly name. The signatories in this section are family heads, not individuals: each name represents a covenant commitment binding the entire priestly house.
4: Ḥattûš Šeḇanyāh (10:4). Hattush appears in the list of returnees with Ezra (Ezra 8:2) — a Davidic descendant who returned with Ezra. His presence among the priests signals either a broader use of the priestly list or a distinct Hattush from the same family. Shebaniah appears multiple times in Neh 9-10 as a Levitical/priestly name.
5: Mallûk Ḥārîm (10:5). Harim (dedicated/consecrated) is one of the families listed in both the priestly return inventory (Ezra 2:39; Neh 7:42) and the Ezra 10 intermarriage list (10:31) — a priestly family with a documented post-exilic history of covenant struggle and covenant renewal. The recurrence of these names across multiple lists establishes the historicity of the community's institutional memory.
6: Meremôt ʿOḇadyāh (10:6). Meremoth son of Uriah the priest is attested as the official who weighed the temple vessels brought by Ezra (Ezra 8:33) — giving him a specific administrative role in the restoration. His presence on the covenant document locates his signature within a broader career of priestly-administrative service to the post-exilic community.
7: ʾAḇiyyāh Miyyāmîn (10:7). Abijah (YHWH is father) is one of the 24 priestly divisions established by David (1 Chr 24:10) — the connection to the Davidic liturgical organization grounds the post-exilic priestly structure in the pre-exilic temple service. The renewal of these divisions in Neh 12:4, 17 shows continuity between the Solomonic/Davidic temple orders and the restoration community.
8: Bilgay Šemayāh — ʾēlleh haKōhănîm (10:8). The priestly signatory list closes with the descriptor: these are the priests. The list of 21 priestly family heads in 10:2-8 represents the institutional hierarchy of the restoration priesthood. Their covenant commitment is simultaneously personal and corporate: each family bound itself and its descendants to the covenant terms that follow.
9: Yēšûaʿ ben-ʾAzanyāh (10:9). The Levitical list opens with Joshua son of Azaniah — a different Joshua from the high priest Jeshua/Joshua of Ezra 2–3. The Levitical branch of the covenant community is represented by its own family heads, distinct from the priestly houses of 10:2-8. The separation of priestly and Levitical signatories reflects the institutional differentiation between the two service orders.
10: Šeḇanyāh Hôḏîyāh (10:10). Shebaniah (YHWH has returned/restored) appears multiple times in the Nehemiah narrative — as a Levite in the Torah-reading assembly (9:4-5) and here among the Levitical covenant signatories. The recurrence of the same names across liturgical and legal contexts establishes the personnel continuity of the Levitical leadership through the reform period.
11: Mîḵāh Rēhôḇ (10:11). The name Micah (who is like YHWH?) echoes the prophet and the Levite of Judges 17-18 — a name with deep resonance in Levitical identity. Rehob (wide/open place) is a name borne by several figures in OT narrative. The Levitical names in this list tend toward theological meaning: they encode covenant identity in their etymology.
12: Ḥašaḇyāh Baqqûq Šeḇanyāh (10:12). Hashabiah (YHWH has considered/reckoned) is a prominent Levitical name in Chronicles and Nehemiah — Levites of this name appear in the temple-service lists (1 Chr 6:45; 9:14; Neh 11:15; 12:21, 24). Multiple Levites share the name, reflecting the family-lineage pattern of the Levitical service orders.
13: ʾēlleh halayyᵉwiyyim — rāʾšê halayyᵉwiyyim (10:13). The Levitical signatory section closes with the designation: these are the Levites — heads of the Levites. The parallel structure to the priestly list's close (10:8) reflects the formal legal drafting of the covenant document: each signatory group is introduced, listed, and closed. The symmetry signals the document's deliberate construction as a legal instrument.
14: rāʾšê hāʿām — heads of the people (10:14). The lay-leadership list (10:14-27) is the third category of covenant signatories: after priests and Levites, the tribal family heads. These are the civil leaders of the non-priestly community — the lay aristocracy of Jerusalem and Judah. Their covenant commitment covers the population outside the cult personnel, making the covenant inclusive of the entire community structure.
15: Bunni ʿAzgāḏ (10:15). Azgad (Gad is strong) — one of the lay families listed in the Ezra 2/Nehemiah 7 return inventories (Ezra 2:12: 1,222 sons of Azgad). The large number of returnees under this family name indicates a substantial lay community. Their representative's signature on the covenant document binds a significant portion of the lay population.
16: ʾAḏônîyāh Bighwāy ʿAḏîn (10:16). Adonijah (YHWH is Lord) is a theologically loaded name — shared with David's son and with a different leader in the return (Ezra 2:13). Bigvai appears in the return lists as another substantial family (Ezra 2:14: 2,056). The presence of family heads from the major returnee groups confirms that the covenant document represents the institutional leadership of the restoration community.
17: ʾĀṭēr Ḥizziqîyāh ʿAzzûr (10:17). Ater (bound/closed) — a family represented in the gatekeepers of the return (Ezra 2:42: 98 gatekeepers). The laypeople's list includes service families as well as large tribal groups, showing the covenant's breadth: it encompasses the full social hierarchy from tribal leaders to temple servants.
18: Hôḏîyāh Hāšûm Bēṣay (10:18). Hashum — another return-list family (Ezra 2:19: 223 men). The repetition of family names across Ezra 2, Nehemiah 7, and Nehemiah 10 creates a documentary thread: the same families who returned from Babylon under Zerubbabel are now, two generations later, sealing a covenant document. The continuity of family identity across the generations of the restoration is itself theologically significant.
19: Ḥārîph ʿAnātôt (10:19). Anathoth — the hometown of Jeremiah (Jer 1:1) — is here a family name, not the town. The association of this name with the most prominent prophetic figure of the exile period may be intentional: the community signing this covenant document stands in the line of those who heard Jeremiah's call to covenant faithfulness.
20: Maggîyāš Mešullām (10:20). Meshullam is one of the most common names in the return lists — multiple Meshullams appear across Ezra-Nehemiah (Ezra 8:16; 10:15, 29; Neh 3:4, 6, 30; 6:18; 8:4; 11:7, 11; 12:13, 16). The name means friend/ally — a name carried by diverse individuals from priestly, Levitical, and lay lines.
21: Mᵉšēzaḇʾēl Ṣāḏôq (10:21). Meshezabel (God has delivered) — a theophoric name expressing confidence in divine redemption. Zadok is the great priestly name of Israelite history — the high priest of David's era (2 Sam 15:24-29) whose line retained the Jerusalem priesthood through the monarchy. The presence of a Zadok among the lay leaders (not in the priestly list) suggests a non-priestly bearer of the historic name.
22: Pellāṭyāh Ḥānān ʾĂnāyāh (10:22). Pelaiah (YHWH is marvelous/distinguishes) — the theological naming pattern continues throughout the lay list. The consistent Yah-bearing names across the signatory list reflect the covenantal identity of the community: they bear in their very names the assertion of YHWH's redemptive activity.
23: Hôšēaʿ Ḥananyāh Ḥaššûḇ (10:23). Hoshea — the name of the last king of Israel (2 Kgs 17:1-6) and also the original name of Joshua (Num 13:8) — here continues its currency as a lay family name. Hasshub (reckoning, esteem) appears among the wall-builders (Neh 3:11, 23) — a family active in the reconstruction before the covenant renewal.
24: Hallôḥēš Pilhāʾ Šôḇēq (10:24). Hallohesh (the whisperer/enchanter) is an unusual name — also borne by a wall-builder (Neh 3:12). The diversity of names in the lay list — from theophoric to nature-based to occupational — reflects the sociological breadth of the covenant community's leadership.
25: Rêhûm Ḥašaḇnāh (10:25). Rehum appears in multiple contexts in Ezra-Nehemiah: an opponent in Ezra 4:8-9 and a Levitical family in the return (Neh 12:3). The coincidence of names with opponents of the restoration in some cases and participants in others reflects the complex social history of the Persian-period Judean community.
26: ʾĂḥîyāh Ḥānān ʾĀnān (10:26). Ahijah (YHWH is brother) — the name of the prophet who announced the division of the kingdom to Jeroboam (1 Kgs 11:29-40). Its recurrence as a lay family name in the post-exilic community keeps the prophetic tradition alive in family identity. The naming of children after prophets and judges was a form of covenant memory.
27: Mallûk Ḥārîm Baʿanāh — ʾēlleh śārê hāʿām (10:27). The lay signatory list closes with: these are the princes/leaders of the people. The triple-tier structure of the covenant document — priests (10:2-8), Levites (10:9-13), lay leaders (10:14-27) — mirrors the social organization of the post-exilic community and ensures comprehensive institutional commitment to the covenant's terms.
28: wᵉkol-hannibdāl mēʿammê hāʾărāṣôt ʾel-tôrat hāʾĕlōhîm — all who separated themselves from the peoples of the lands to the law of God (10:28). The nip'al of bāḏal (to separate oneself) here defines the community's membership criterion: those who have enacted the covenant separation of ch 9–10. The phrase extends the covenant beyond the formal signatories to include all who have made the same separation — spouses, children, gatekeepers, singers, Nethinim. kol-yôḏēaʿ mēḇîn — everyone who has knowledge and understanding — the covenant applies to those with capacity to understand covenant obligation, not merely by heredity.
29: ḥăzāq bᵉʾaḥêhem ʾaddîrêhem — they joined their brothers, their nobles (10:29). ḥāzaq bᵉ (to hold firmly to, join oneself firmly to) is the verb of covenant adherence: clinging to rather than loose association. The legal formula that follows — ʾālāh ûšᵉḇûʿāh (curse and oath) — is the full covenant-sealing instrument: the ʾālāh (self-imprecation; curse if the covenant is violated) combined with the šᵉḇûʿāh (sworn oath). The bilateral structure invokes both divine sanction and personal obligation: breaking the covenant brings the curse; keeping it fulfills the oath.
30: lōʾ nittēn bᵉnôtênû lᵉʿammê hāʾāreṣ — we will not give our daughters to the peoples of the land (10:30). The intermarriage prohibition is the first substantive covenant pledge — the lesson directly drawn from the Ezra 9-10 crisis. wᵉlōʾ niqaḥ bᵉnôtêhem lᵉḇānênû — and we will not take their daughters for our sons — the bilateral formulation covers both directions of intermarriage. The covenant pledge is forward-looking: the community binds itself for future generations, not merely addressing the past crisis.
31: wᵉʿammê hāʾāreṣ hamméḇîʾîm ʾet-hammiqqāḥôt wᵉḵol-šeḇer bᵉyôm haššabbāt lišbōr — if the peoples of the land bring wares or any grain on the Sabbath to sell (10:31). The Sabbath-commerce covenant builds on Nehemiah's later enforcement narrative (13:15-22) — the covenant pledge precedes the enforcement crisis, or both covenant and crisis reflect the same ongoing problem. niqaḥ mēhem bᵉšabbāt ûḇᵉyôm qōḏeš — we will not buy from them on the Sabbath or on a holy day — the prohibition is on purchasing as well as selling: both sides of a commercial transaction violate Sabbath rest. winᵉṭōš ʾet-haššānāh haššᵉḇîʿît — and we will forgo the produce of the seventh year — the Sabbath-year release of Lev 25 is included alongside the weekly Sabbath: the covenant commits to the full Sabbath-framework of Israel's law.
32: wᵉheʿĕmadnû ʿālênû miṣwōt — we set commandments upon ourselves (10:32). heʿĕmîḏ ʿal (to set upon, to impose on oneself) is an unusual reflexive construction for self-imposed obligation — the community is voluntarily taking on the temple tax. šᵉlîšit haššeqel baššānāh lᵉʿăḇôḏat bêt ʾĕlōhênû — a third of a shekel per year for the service of the house of our God — the Mosaic tradition prescribed a half-shekel (Exod 30:13-16) as the census tax; the reduction to a third-shekel may reflect Persian-era economic conditions or a deliberately modest community standard.
33: lᵉleḥem hammaʿăreḵet ûminḥat hattāmîḏ ûlᵉʿôlat hattāmîḏ — for the showbread, the regular grain offering, and the regular burnt offering (10:33). The list of temple service categories the community funds with the annual contribution — leḥem hammaʿăreḵet (bread of the arrangement/Presence; showbread), the daily minḥāh (grain offering), and the daily ʿôlāh (burnt offering) — represents the regular cult operations of the Second Temple. The Sabbaths, new moons, appointed feasts, holy things, and sin offerings are all included: the community is committing to the complete liturgical funding of the restored temple service.
34: wᵉhaggôrālôt hippalnû ʿal-qurbat hāʾēṣîm — we cast lots for the wood offering (10:34). The qurban hāʾēṣîm (wood offering, lit. drawing-near of wood) is not explicitly mentioned in the Pentateuchal law — the Temple Scroll from Qumran (11QT) and Josephus (War 2.17.6) attest a festival of wood-offering in the Second Temple period. The casting of lots (gôrāl) to distribute the responsibility equitably across priestly families and lay families is the covenant community's practical solution to an administrative challenge. The wood offering ensured the continuous supply of fuel for the altar fire (Lev 6:9-13).
35: ûlᵉhāḇîʾ ʾet-bikkûrê ʾadmātênû ûbikkûrê kol-pᵉrî kol-ʿēṣ — to bring the firstfruits of our ground and the firstfruits of all fruit of every tree (10:35). bikkûrîm (firstfruits) is the Pentateuchal term for the first-and-best of the agricultural harvest dedicated to YHWH (Exod 23:19; 34:26; Num 18:12-13; Deut 26:1-11). The dedication of the firstfruits is the covenanted acknowledgment that the land and its produce belong to YHWH: the first portion represents and consecrates the whole. The Feast of Weeks (Shavuot) was the primary firstfruits festival — the community here pledges its annual observance.
36: wᵉʾet-bᵉḵôrôt bānênû ûḇhemtênû — and the firstborn of our sons and our cattle (10:36). The bᵉḵôr (firstborn) dedication follows from the Exodus firstborn logic: YHWH claimed all firstborn human and animal at the Passover (Exod 13:2, 11-15) and the dedication/redemption of firstborn is the perpetual liturgical re-enactment of that claim. kaKKātûḇ battôrāh — as it is written in the law — the phrase grounds the firstborn obligation in specific Pentateuchal citation (Exod 13:2; 34:19-20; Num 18:15-16): the covenant is a return to what was already written, not new legislation.
37: wᵉʾet-rêʾšît ʿărîsōtênû wᵉterûmōtênû — and the firstfruits of our dough and our contributions (10:37). ʿărîsāh (dough/coarse meal; the first portion of grain ground into dough) is the term in Num 15:20-21 for the dough-cake offered to YHWH from each batch: the first of the processed grain as distinct from the unprocessed firstfruits of the field. terûmāh (contribution, wave-offering; the portion lifted/set apart for YHWH) is the technical term for the dedicated portion of diverse goods — grain, oil, wine — set aside for the priests (Num 18:8-12). The layered agricultural vocabulary in 10:35-37 covers every stage of agricultural production from field to processed food.
38: wᵉhakkōhēn ben-ʾAhărōn yihyeh ʿim halayyᵉwiyyim bᵉʿaśēr halayyᵉwiyyim — a priest of the sons of Aaron shall be with the Levites when the Levites receive the tithe (10:38). The priestly oversight of the Levitical tithe collection is grounded in Num 18:26-28: the Levites tithe their tithe to the priests. The covenant pledge specifies the accompanying priest to ensure accountability — a detail that reflects the administrative realities of post-exilic temple finance. wᵉhalayyᵉwiyyim yaʿălû ʾet-maʿaśar hammaʿăśēr lᵉḇêt ʾĕlōhênû — the Levites bring the tithe of the tithe to the house of our God — the double-tithe system maintained the temple treasury's share even as the primary tithe went to the Levites.
39: lōʾ naʿăzōḇ ʾet-bêt ʾĕlōhênû — we will not neglect the house of our God (10:39). The covenant climax is expressed in a single negative commitment: lōʾ naʿăzōḇ (we will not forsake/neglect). ʿāzaḇ (to forsake, leave, abandon) is the most theologically loaded verb in the covenant vocabulary: it is what Israel repeatedly did to YHWH (9:26: wayyaʿazᵉḇûk) and what YHWH refused to do to Israel (9:31: lōʾ-ʿăzaḇtām). The community's final pledge mirrors YHWH's own covenant faithfulness: as YHWH refused to forsake them, so they commit not to forsake his house. The covenant document ends where it began — in the vocabulary of the divine-human abandonment/faithfulness dialectic that ran through the entire Levitical prayer.
